text: The surgeon demonstrates a robotic tap inguinal hernia repair in a patient with a prior robotic prostatectomy. The right indirect inguinal hernia is visible, with the sigmoid epiploca adhered to the peritoneum medial to the inferior epigastric vessels. The surgeon assesses the defect size and overlap needed for the mesh, then begins the peritoneal incision, creating a flap between the peritoneum and transversalis fascia. The retro-rectus space is accessed to identify the inferior epigastric vessels. The surgeon moves medially into the space of Retzius, then laterally, dividing layers of the transversalis fascia and investing fascia to stay close to the peritoneum, maneuvering around the sac and testicular vessels.

The surgeon carefully dissects the Shiozaki layer to separate fat from the peritoneum, avoiding injury to the genital femoral nerve and lateral femoral cutaneous nerve. The testicular vessels are visible and must be preserved. The surgeon uses short bursts of cautery and retracts tissue with the left hand to create areolar tissue, ensuring they are in the correct plane. The right medial umbilical ligament is divided to increase working space medially, and the indirect sac is reduced with precise traction and cautery application.

The surgeon uses short bursts of cautery to maintain hemostasis while carefully dissecting small vessels off the sac. The left hand pulls to the nine o'clock position, spreading the tissue layers for better visibility. The VAS deferens is visible, allowing for more aggressive dissection around the inferior epigastric vessels. The left hand retracts the bladder and fat away from the rectus muscle, revealing white areolar separation planes.

The surgeon continues to peel away the peritoneum, carefully avoiding injury to the inferior gastric vessels by hugging the peritoneum. Short bursts of energy are used to visualize tissue planes. As the dissection deepens, the surgeon is cautious of the external iliac vein and femoral vein potentially being pulled medially. The surgeon identifies fat to ensure veins are not in the dissection field and prepares to free up the peritoneum. Dissection proceeds through natural planes between fat layers, using scissors to push against tissue to detect bone. The surgeon identifies a whitish structure as the bladder and uses sharp dissection with short bursts of cautery, avoiding excessive energy near the bladder and hugging the bony structures of the pelvis.

The surgeon uses the left hand to pull and reveal a white, foamy areolar layer, allowing precise cutting with the right hand. Dense tissue is visible near the pubis, and the bladder is clearly seen. Hemostasis is maintained by stopping any bleeding and using gauze to absorb blood. The surgeon carefully peels the peritoneum away from the testicular vessel to ensure proper mesh placement. The medial umbilical ligament is exposed, and the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve and genital femoral nerve are visible next to the iliac artery. The surgeon exchanges the scissors for a mega suture cut needle driver to handle the pre-inserted 15x10 anatomical Pritex Pro Grip mesh, unfolding it to position the seam about two centimeters from the internal ring.

The surgeon ensures the lower flap is properly positioned in the space of Retzius against the pubis and Cooper's ligament, confirming adequate posterior coverage. The surgeon checks for any peritoneum behind the posterior flap of the mesh before proceeding with the routine closure of the flap. The left hand assists in pulling the needle out and positioning it for the right hand to continue suturing.

The surgeon uses a 3-0 180 V-Loc suture to close the peritoneum, throwing the suture four to six times before pulling it through. The suture is cut flush to the peritoneum to prevent any tail from sticking out. A hole in the peritoneum is closed with figure-of-eight 3-0 Vicryl sutures, completing the operation.
